comment.body:
Always be patient, and avoid trading for only the sake of it.
Without conception, without plan, without thinking, there is no longer-term growth. 
Be patient with the growth.
Market won't let you to be millionaire overnight.

Most ppl fall for the mistake to try to trade everything like if somebody would put a gun to your head to do. But if I would ask, why you made this or that decision, you could not answer properly.
This is a mental trap. I tell you what I mean by that.
You have $800 on your account. You go trading and you lose $300
Next day you win again $500
Next day you lose $200, but you get a successful 'revenge trading' and win $400
You get overconfident and lose $700 on the next day. 
And finally on the last day you can gain $300.

Now listen...
 -300 + 500 - 200 + 400 - 700 + 300 = 0

Think about it...

comment.body: Look at on balance volumes versus Cumulative volume deltas. Tradingview should have an indicator for this. So OBV is increasing but CVD is actually flat which you could take as selling into strength. As far as what vol is doing, HV20 is at 10% and decreasing. The more it decreases the higher the probability of a vol increase (if there is 0% volatility the market wouldn't move lol)

comment.body:
Thank you for the CBOE link Alma, quick question about linear vs logistic regression. Why do quants or furu’s like to harp on linear regression for markets instead of logistic? US markets seem to exhibit logarithmic behavior especially over the longer term view as opposed linear behavior, no? Or am I incorrect in that assumption/observation? 

The S-curve would seem to fit better as markets are themselves a composite of sine waves (can link studies related to wavelet convolution and FFT’s for that) than a straight line linear model would from what I’m seeing but I’m not sure if I’m just mis-interpreting the data

comment.body:
@Tim Lam and also bcs there is no free lunch...
convexity costs time premium.
theta/gamma ratio tells you how much time decay you pay for one unit of convexity. 
if vol up, positive convexity comes with steeper time‐decay. and vice versa. 
but the interesting is that convexity has a price in terms of theta. so more positive convexity across the map = more theta decay
comment.raw_body:
${0} and also bcs there is no free lunch...
convexity costs time premium.
theta/gamma ratio tells you how much time decay you pay for one unit of convexity. 
if vol up, positive convexity comes with steeper time‐decay. and vice versa. 
but the interesting is that convexity has a price in terms of theta. so more positive convexity across the map = more theta decay

comment.body:
I am rolling the short legs of my calendars since two weeks. Very nice profit indeed.

I bought OTM calls in July at the low on Friday. Today they are up 40%. This is my long gamma for the month. Scalping short futures around it, based on the daily levels. I bought behind the FED meeting date. That vol should stay elevated as closer we get to the Fed date.

comment.body:
I read Almas article and look at dealer position. I focus on the biggest OI and gamma levels before the opening.

Today I used the choppiness between 6180-6190 for couple of scalps (short from the top, close at the bottom). It was around Almas centroid. 

The biggest negative gamma was at 6205. The area below 6190 was a strip of positive gamma. Additional Alma mentioned the „long fly“ exposure, meaning long gamma below and above and short gamma at the center. You could expect choppiness at the body of the fly.

That area was where the effect of negative gamma from 6205 and the positive gamma from the strip below 6190 clashed together.

Sure enough we closed exactly at the price where the biggest OI at the market opening was.

comment.body:
Mm are long a straddle between the strikes of the IC. I am counting with a slow day (inside day).
I have to correct your second sentence. Mm are never long vega. They are vega neutral to protect them against vol risk. They are long vol convexity (vomma/volga) and protect the market against vol spikes.
In that case you mentioned, (dropping) Mm would sell vomma/volga through /VX futures, or OTM puts. This caps any vol spike and protect (to some extend) a fall in the market.

comment.body:
Hi Alma
During my after-hours review of yesterday’s note, I was struck by how precisely some of your levels played out—support materialised almost exactly at 6173 before the rebound, and the close was neatly capped just under 6205. I’ve only been a subscriber for about a month and I’m still working through the books you recommended, so stitching everything together into one coherent framework is proving a bit challenging. I’m eager to learn, though, and would really appreciate any additional colour you can share on how you arrived at those levels.

comment.body:
I am long OTM calls from July 31 and short futures against it, making me essentially long gamma. If the market moves up, the calls profit and the futures make a loss. If the market goes down, the futures make a profit and the calls lose. Since I am positive gamma, the rate of change is favorable for my position. I lose, if the market does not move at all.
I am not exposed to directional movements in any way (delta) and protected against crazy moves, or headlines, but I pay every day theta to hold that position. My profits are made with realized vol (gamma) being higher then expected vol (IV).
To protect the calls from losing value in a drop in vol, I bought the expiration after the Fed date. Since the market is nervous about decisions from the Fed, vol stays bid or even rizes behind that date.
Since we are in a spot up / vol up regime and vol hit a floor, the movement in vol is favorable for long options. 
If spot goes up and vol too, OTM calls make profits on both. As one leg is a future, I can close and open that leg as I like. If we hit a daily support, I can close the future (losses from the calls were reduced) and let the calls profit from the recovery. I short the future again, when a daily resistance is hit and lock in the gains from the calls (as the futures will make a profit, if the market falls).

comment.body:
@Tim Lam and also bcs there is no free lunch...
convexity costs time premium.
theta/gamma ratio tells you how much time decay you pay for one unit of convexity. 
if vol up, positive convexity comes with steeper time‐decay. and vice versa. 
but the interesting is that convexity has a price in terms of theta. so more positive convexity across the map = more theta decay
